comingSoon.title: Pardon Our Dust!
database.availableLicenses.description: In 2024 a new law increased access to liquor licenses in several parts of Boston. Through this law the city will be issuing 5 new on premise liquor licenses to 13 of Boston’s Zip Codes each year for the next 3 years.
database.availableLicenses.title: 2024 Cohort Licenses
database.header.description: Browse through available licenses in each of Boston's ZIP codes, view important information regarding each license type and recent license applications. As well as important dates for license applications.
database.header.nextMeeting: The next Licensing Boarding meeting is: {nextMeetingDate, date, long}
database.header.title: License Database
database.licenseTerminology.description: Boston has several different types of licenses all coming from a variety of different needs, laws, and statues. This can be confusing when navigating through the licensing process for the first time. Below are some basic licensing terms along with their definitions to help navigate the Boston liquor license system.
database.licenseTerminology.allAlcohol.d: The most common of licenses, All Alcohol Licenses allow for its holders to sell all categories of liquor to their patrons (including distilled spirits like gin, vodka, and rum).
These licenses allow establishments to sell wine and malt drinks such as beer and cider as well as so called cordials which are defined as "distilled spirits [mixed] with or over fruits, flowers, plants [etc.] containing no less than two and one-half percent sugar by weight," while limiting the ability to sell distilled spirits and other "hard" liquor.
database.licenseTerminology.transferable: The majority of Boston’s liquor licenses, they are usable throughout the city and can be transferred between business and ownership, with approval by the City and State.
database.licenseTerminology.nonTransfera: Created by special legislations, including the 2024 law, These licenses are beholden to the limitations of their respective legislation. They are non-transferable, meaning that should a business close or ownership change, they revert back to the City.
database.recentApplications.title: Recent License Applications By Zip Code:
database.recentApplications.granted.desc: Licenses that have been granted by the City of Boston from an application. These licenses can be used by their operators.
database.recentApplications.expired.desc: Applications that have passed the amount of time for the City of Boston to consider them. Applicants will have to reapply.
database.recentApplications.deferred.des: License Applications that have yet to be considered by the Licensing Board. (This applies to applications before the next meeting)
home.aboutUs.title: Who We Are:
home.aboutUs.weAre.part1: We are a collection of restaurant owners, bartenders, chefs, and patrons; Just Like You. We understand how hard it is navigating the red tape of liquor licenses in Boston especially as a newcomer.
home.aboutUs.weAre.part2: From tracking availability, understanding the application process, to the potential hidden costs of obtaining one: there is a lot to keep track of. We’ve made it our mission to demystify the process, and help you get your business off the ground as painlessly as possible.
home.caseStudy.part1: Getting a Liquor License in Boston has long meant being part of an "In Club" of restaurant owners, hospitality companies, and lawyers who've mastered the process. It has historically meant newcomers to the space have struggled to obtain these artificially scarce licenses, and has created droughts in neighborhoods all over the city. Our research has shown a direct correlation between how white a neighborhood is and how many liquor licenses it has.
home.caseStudy.part2: Recent changes have made moves to improve access to Licenses. A new law passed in 2024 aims to increase access to liquor licenses, creating licenses for specific ZIP codes. However, this is just the start. Tracking available licenses is still difficult, and access to accurate, up-to-date information is often out of reach.
home.caseStudy.part3: With this site we are working to create a more equitable restaurant ecosystem with readily available, free, and accurate information for all and enabling restaurant operators to track and understand what Licenses are available to them.
home.caseStudy.title: Why is this important?
home.caseStudy.video.title: License to Succeed - Liquor License Informational Video
home.extraWork.title: Check out more of our work!
home.hero.heading: Boston’s Liquor License system can be confusing. We’re here to help you navigate it and ensure your business can succeed.
home.hero.title: Upscale bar with old-school industrial bulbs as a decoration
home.quote: ...working to create a more equitable restaurant ecosystem for all...

resources.title: Application Resources and Guides
resources.text: Resources and guides through the liquor license application process, from the city of Boston to industry veterans, and leading companies in the industry. These guides will provide step by step instructions that will walk through the application process for any potential licensee.
resources.cityGuides.title: City of Boston Guides and Resources
resources.cityGuides.text: The City of Boston's Licensing Board (BLB) has provided several guides to help new and existing businesses obtain, update, and change their Licenses. The Licensing Board website provides resources on all types of actions for Licenses, most importantly including a step by step guide going over the application process. They have also helpfully uploaded a video to the City's YouTube channel walking through the application steps.
resources.offsiteGuide.title: Offsite Licensing Application Guide
resources.offsiteGuide.text: A comprehensive guide to the application process for obtaining a Liquor License in the City of Boston. Assembled by industry veterans and license advocates, this guide provides detailed step by step instructions for prospective Boston restaurateurs with the knowledge to move through the application process with ease.
resources.toast.title: Toast Massachusetts Licensing Resource and Information
resources.toast.text: A general guide to the state level process of obtaining a Liquor License. Giving a general overview of what to expect when going through the application process as well as general information regarding local restrictions and fees that might apply during the licensing process.
resources.toast.website: How to Get a Liquor License in Massachusetts
resources.agencies.title: Agencies Involved in the Application Process
resources.agencies.text: The application process for obtaining a Liquor License in Boston requires any prospective licensee to contact several different agencies and offices to obtain a license. Here are some quick links to the most important agencies and how they affect the application process.
resources.agencies.blb.title: Boston Licensing Board
resources.agencies.blb.text: The agency which is responsible for the granting and regulation of alcohol and food licenses inside the city of Boston. All Liquor licenses go across their desks alongside Common Victualler (CV) Licenses. They are the ones who will be granting the new 2024 licenses during their meetings.
resources.agencies.abcc.title: Alcoholic Beverages Control Commission
resources.agencies.abcc.text: The Massachusetts agency which oversees the sale, transportation, possession, purchasing, and manufacturing of alcoholic beverages. Under the state treasury office they are mainly dealt with by the Boston Licensing Board and interactions with licensees is limited.
resources.agencies.ons.title: Office of Neighborhood Services
resources.agencies.ons.text: The City of Boston's neighborhood support agency, which focuses on providing support and assistance to the neighborhoods of Boston. For the Liquor Licensing process they are responsible for connecting potential businesses with members of the community in preparation for being granted a Liquor License.
resources.basicResources.title: Basic Resources
resources.basicResources.text: Below are some general resources to help prospective license applicants or even general restaurateurs to better understand liquor licenses in Boston and how the different agencies or requirements might affect the application process.
resources.basicResources.glossary.title: License Glossary
resources.basicResources.glossary.restri: Restricted, also known as Non-Transferable licenses, are a catch-all term for any Boston Licenses that contain special restrictions and are not able to be transferred to another business and/or location. These licenses are created by special legislation, including the most recent law in 2024. They are beholden to the limitations of their respective legislation (see below for details) and should a business close or ownership change, they revert back to the City.
resources.basicResources.glossary.transf: The opposite side of Licenses in Boston, any License that does not have restrictions preventing them from being transferred to another business and/or location are considered Transferable. This includes the majority of all licenses in the city, with some being created from legislation and many issued prior to 2006.
resources.basicResources.glossary.onPrem: Licenses granted for the sale and consumption of alcohol at various establishments and businesses with the intention of customers and clients to consume on site. Otherwise known as “pouring licenses” or “Section 12 Licenses” under Mass state law. These Licenses are often held by businesses such as restaurants, hotels, clubs, and any other business where consumption occurs. Many of these are tied to a “Common Victualler” license which is required to serve food, which are also issued by the Boston Licensing Board.
resources.basicResources.glossary.offPre: Commonly known as a “package store license” or a “Section 15 license” under Mass state law, they are defined as Licenses for businesses that are considered “package goods stores, convenience stores, supermarkets, grocery stores, food stores, wine shops, and so-called big box retailers such as warehouse clubs.”
resources.basicResources.glossary.wineMa: An On Premise License that permits the sale of Beer, Wine, and other low alcoholic beverages to their clientele. Under state law Wine is defined as “all fermented alcoholic beverages made from fruits, flowers, herbs, or vegetables and containing not more than twenty-four percent of alcohol by volume.” Beer however falls under the Malt category which looks at all alcoholic beverages manufactured or produced by the process of brewing or fermentation of malt, with or without cereal grains or fermentable sugars, or of hops, and containing not more than twelve percent of alcohol by weight.
resources.basicResources.glossary.cordia: A special license type which can be added on top of a Wine & Malt License. These licenses are a compromise that allows businesses to offer a wider range of alcoholic selections on their menus. Businesses with a Wine & Malt license can choose to petition to add Liqueurs to their license and there is no limit to these licenses outside of the Standard Wine & Malt. For the purposes of this License, Cordials are considered any “Liqueur or cordial”, all alcoholic beverages manufactured or produced by mixing distilled spirits with or over fruits, flowers, plants etc., containing no less than two and one-half percent sugar by weight.
resources.basicResources.glossary.allAlc: The more common type of License in the city, permitting the holding establishment the sale of all alcoholic beverages defined by the state as any liquid intended for human consumption as a beverage and containing one half of one percent (0.05%) or more alcohol by volume.
resources.basicResources.otherLicenses.l: Created the first set of Restricted Licenses in the City of Boston. Limited the restrictions to Boston Redevelopment Agency designated areas such as Boston Main Streets districts, Empowerment zones, Neighborhoods in the city's urban renewal program, and the South Boston Waterfront. It created between 10-25 restricted all-alcohol licenses and 30 Wine & Malt, with special inclusions for 5 going to Hotels, 10 going to current holders looking to upgrade with the rest going to the restricted areas along with 30 Wine & Malt licenses. When these licenses return to the City, they may be allocated to anywhere within the zones defined by the BRA in the law, including the South Boston Waterfront.

A special case law, in 2012 created a special status for licenses going to restaurants in the Boston Logan Airport area that made the licenses restricted to the airport in exchange for not counting them as part of the municipal quota for licenses. It also included conditions for the transfer and/or removal of municipal quota licenses that were in use and return them to the city in exchange for a new restricted license. The nature of this law also seems to indicate that there is no cap to licenses issued in the airport.

Created the first neighborhood restricted licenses, specifically targeting the zoning districts of Dorchester, East Boston, Hyde Park, Jamaica Plain, Mattapan, Mission Hill and Roxbury as designated by the Boston Zoning Commission as well as in the areas designated by the Boston Redevelopment Authority as “main street districts”. These licenses differ from the earlier licenses created in 2006 as they are bound specifically to the neighborhoods they were issued in and cannot be transferred out of them.

The most recent license type to be introduced to the city of Boston. Created to serve 13 Zip Codes as designated through input from Neighborhood Associations, Main Streets, City Councilors, and the State House. Like the 2014 License block, these licenses are restricted to the area where they are issued and cannot be transferred between Zip Codes. The law also has provisions for exceptions outside of the zip codes including the Oak Square neighborhood and set aside licenses for community spaces and theaters. Released over a period of 3 years, the law will create 225 new licenses for various businesses around the city. Each Zip Code will have 5 new licenses released each year for the following 3 years. Alongside 15 for theaters and community spaces, 3 for Oak Square, and a further 12 standard unrestricted licenses.
resources.basicResources.otherLicenses.u: The default liquor license in Boston, part of the State of Massachusetts Quota system which puts a limit on the amount of licenses the city of Boston can have within its city limits. These licenses are usable throughout the city and can be transferred between business and ownership, with approval by City and State licensing agencies. This method of licenses has led to an increase in aftermarket prices for unused licenses and has shut out many small business owners from being able to acquire licenses. The current number of these licenses is hard to track due to their nature but according to estimates there are approximately 1000 currently in circulation.
